system_prompt: The user has deep knowledge of contemporary art, photography, and design. Discusses aesthetic frameworks with precision — references specific movements, structural principles, and material strategies. Draws connections across disciplines (architecture, fashion, philosophy, film). Has strong opinions on composition and cultural positioning. Expects critical engagement and alternative readings, not survey-level overviews. Comfortable being challenged and revising positions.
user_prompt: Why do some luxury brands maintain value over decades while others collapse?
response_text:
The core distinction isn't brand management — it's whether the object precedes the sign or the sign precedes the object. Hermès survives because the Birkin is a saddler's artifact first, a status symbol second. The craft logic is materially embedded: the box-stitch, the specific tannage, the hand-finishing sequence that actually takes years to acquire. When the mythology requires material substantiation, it has a floor. When luxury is constructed entirely through marketing architecture and celebrity placement, there's nothing underneath when the cultural moment shifts. This is why Ed Hardy or early-2000s Versace Jeans Couture collapse — they were semiotic systems without objects, pure sign without referent.

The second mechanism is what I'd call tiered semiotic architecture. Surviving brands maintain strict hierarchy across price tiers rather than flooding the aspirational middle. Hermès runs this deliberately: the Birkin is effectively unobtainable, silk scarves are aspirational-but-achievable, perfume is entry. Each tier draws value from the tier above. Brands collapse when they cannibalize upward — when the logo becomes too accessible, the whole architecture deflates simultaneously. Burberry's check problem in the early 2000s is the textbook case: the monogram migrated to counterfeit market and working-class streetwear simultaneously, and the semiotic damage propagated instantly up-tier.

There's a useful parallel in artistic movements here. Impressionism maintains value across centuries partly because it produced objects with material specificity — actual paint application that can be evaluated on its own terms, independent of any institutional narrative. If Impressionism were a brand identity, it would have dated. The objects support the value against revisionism. The luxury brands that hold share this property: you can strip the mythology from a Patek perpetual calendar and still have a mechanically extraordinary object. Strip the mythology from a logo-centric brand and you have a piece of coated canvas.

The creative director question is more complex. There's a bifurcation: some brands derive their identity from process/craft that predates any individual (Hermès, Patek, Rolex), and these are structurally stable across personnel changes. Others were essentially a single vision — Jil Sander, Helmut Lang, early Calvin Klein — and when the director leaves, you get what the theorist Mark Fisher might have called a "hauntological" brand: the ghost of an aesthetic without the generative source. The interesting case is Bottega Veneta, which nearly collapsed before Daniel Lee stripped it back to intrecciato logic — he recovered value by re-grounding the brand in its material grammar rather than inventing new iconography.

One counterintuitive reading worth pushing: "collapse" is often misread as permanent when it's actually a PE extraction cycle. Coach, Burberry, Gucci have all "collapsed" and repositioned. What looks like brand death is frequently the brand being strip-mined then relaunched with the same name but a new mythology. The collapse is a feature, not a bug — it creates the distance needed for nostalgia repositioning. This complicates the narrative of inherent brand durability, because some "durable" brands are actually serial resurrections that we retrospectively narrate as continuous.

The deepest factor may be what I'd call the inheritance economy. Patek's famous campaign — "you never truly own a Patek Philippe" — isn't just copy. It's stating something structurally true about objects designed to outlive owners, to accumulate patina rather than date, to gain rather than lose legibility over time. This is fundamentally opposed to the planned-obsolescence logic of fashion. Objects that age beautifully (Hermès bridle leather, brushed Oystersteel) have a time-structure built into the material. Objects that date — logo-heavy anything from any era — have an inverse time-structure. The market merely ratifies what the object already is.

What's your angle on this — are you thinking through the valuation logic itself, or is there a specific brand or category you're examining?
